agent_output:
```json
{
  "executive_summary": "This system exhibits five significant architectural anti-patterns that have accumulated over 3 years of organic growth. The most critical issues are the shared database between Order and Courier services (destroying service autonomy), the synchronous Notification fan-in (creating a system-wide single point of failure), and the Reporting Service's direct database reads (creating invisible coupling). Left unaddressed, these patterns will increasingly cause cascading failures, block independent deployments, and make schema evolution dangerous. The remediation plan is sequenced to deliver the highest risk-reduction first while minimizing disruption.",

  "architecture": {
    "components": [
      { "name": "Order Service", "responsibility": "Order lifecycle management", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — shares database with Courier, database read directly by Reporting" },
      { "name": "Courier Service", "responsibility": "Delivery assignment and tracking", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — shares database with Order Service, no independent data ownership" },
      { "name": "Notification Service", "responsibility": "Sending notifications across all channels", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — synchronously coupled to 5 upstream callers, acts as a distributed monolith bottleneck" },
      { "name": "Customer Service", "responsibility": "Customer profile, preferences, addresses, payment methods, order history", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — god endpoint exposes unbounded aggregate, no separation of concerns" },
      { "name": "Payment Service", "responsibility": "Payment processing", "boundaries": "Partially intact, but synchronously coupled to Notification" },
      { "name": "Reporting Service", "responsibility": "Analytics and reporting", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — bypasses Order Service API, reads raw tables directly" }
    ],
    "interfaces": [
      { "from": "Order Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal + behavioral" },
      { "from": "Courier Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal + behavioral" },
      { "from": "Payment Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal + behavioral" },
      { "from": "Customer Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal + behavioral" },
      { "from": "Reporting Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal + behavioral" },
      { "from": "Order Service", "to": "Shared PostgreSQL", "contract": "Direct table access (order_ schema prefix)", "coupling_type": "data + schema" },
      { "from": "Courier Service", "to": "Shared PostgreSQL", "contract": "Direct table access (courier_ schema prefix)", "coupling_type": "data + schema" },
      { "from": "Reporting Service", "to": "Order Service DB", "contract": "Direct SQL reads against order tables", "coupling_type": "data + schema (invisible)" },
      { "from": "Cron Job", "to": "Background Job Endpoints", "contract": "HTTP GET/POST to REST endpoints", "coupling_type": "temporal + transport abuse" },
      { "from": "Customer Service", "to": "Clients", "contract": "Single /api/v1/everything endpoint", "coupling_type": "semantic — over-fetching, god endpoint" }
    ],
    "data_flow": "Currently: services call each other synchronously and share databases directly. Order and Courier both read/write the same PostgreSQL instance. Reporting bypasses the Order API entirely and queries raw tables. All notification-triggering flows block on the Notification Service HTTP response. Background processing is triggered externally via HTTP cron, meaning job execution depends on HTTP infrastructure availability and timeout configuration.",
    "patterns_used": [
      { "pattern": "Shared Database (anti-pattern)", "rationale": "Likely started as a monolith that was split into services without splitting the data" },
      { "pattern": "Synchronous Fan-In (anti-pattern)", "rationale": "Notification was likely the first extracted service — every caller just did HTTP calls" },
      { "pattern": "God Endpoint (anti-pattern)", "rationale": "Probably started as a convenience for a single frontend, then other consumers started depending on it" },
      { "pattern": "API-as-Job-Runner (anti-pattern)", "rationale": "Team likely lacked job infrastructure so reused what they had — HTTP endpoints" },
      { "pattern": "Database Integration (anti-pattern)", "rationale": "Reporting needed data fast and the team took the shortest path" }
    ]
  },

  "domain_model": {
    "bounded_contexts": [
      { "name": "Order Management", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Order", "OrderLine", "Refund"] },
      { "name": "Delivery", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Delivery", "CourierAssignment", "Route"] },
      { "name": "Customer", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["CustomerProfile", "Address", "Preferences"] },
      { "name": "Payment", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["PaymentMethod", "Transaction"] },
      { "name": "Notification", "classification": "generic", "aggregates": ["NotificationRequest", "DeliveryChannel", "Template"] },
      { "name": "Reporting", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["Report", "Materialized Views"] }
    ],
    "context_map": [
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Delivery", "relationship": "Customer-Supplier — Order publishes events, Delivery consumes. Currently VIOLATED by shared DB." },
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Reporting", "relationship": "Should be Published Language (events/API). Currently VIOLATED by direct DB integration." },
      { "upstream": "All Services", "downstream": "Notification", "relationship": "Should be Open Host Service with async events. Currently synchronous coupling." },
      { "upstream": "Customer", "downstream": "Order Management", "relationship": "Conformist — Order uses customer data as-is" },
      { "upstream": "Payment", "downstream": "Order Management", "relationship": "Customer-Supplier — Payment confirms transactions for Order" }
    ]
  },

  "trade_offs": [
    {
      "decision": "Split shared Order/Courier database into separate datastores",
      "gains": "Independent deployability, independent schema evolution, failure isolation, clear data ownership",
      "sacrifices": "Cross-service queries become harder, eventual consistency replaces ACID joins, migration effort is high",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Schema-level isolation with strict access control (half-measure, doesn't solve coupling)", "Shared database with published views as the contract (reduces coupling but maintains operational dependency)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Replace synchronous Notification calls with async event-driven messaging",
      "gains": "Eliminates single point of failure, callers no longer block on notification delivery, natural retry/DLQ semantics",
      "sacrifices": "Adds messaging infrastructure (broker), notification delivery is eventually consistent, harder to debug in dev",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Fire-and-forget HTTP (partial fix but still couples on availability)", "Sidecar/outbox pattern (good but higher implementation complexity)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Break /everything endpoint into focused resource endpoints",
      "gains": "Clients fetch only what they need, independent scaling of sub-resources, clearer API contracts, better cacheability",
      "sacrifices": "Clients making multiple calls (mitigated by BFF or GraphQL), breaking change for existing consumers",
      "alternatives_considered": ["GraphQL layer over the monolith endpoint (adds complexity but preserves backward compat)", "Sparse fieldsets / JSON:API style filtering (band-aid, doesn't fix the aggregate boundary problem)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Move background jobs to a proper job/queue system",
      "gains": "Retry semantics, dead letter handling, concurrency control, no HTTP timeout issues, proper observability",
      "sacrifices": "New infrastructure component to operate, migration of existing cron entries",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Keep HTTP endpoints but add idempotency keys and timeouts (lipstick on a pig)", "Serverless functions triggered by scheduler (viable if on cloud, adds cold start considerations)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Replace Reporting's direct DB reads with a dedicated read model / data pipeline",
      "gains": "Order Service owns its schema freely, Reporting gets optimized read model, decoupled deployment",
      "sacrifices": "Data latency (seconds to minutes), pipeline infrastructure to maintain, initial data backfill",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Published views owned by Order Service (simple but still schema-coupled)", "CDC stream (Debezium) to replicate into Reporting's own store (best decoupling but highest infra cost)"]
    }
  ],

  "flexibility_points": [
    { "area": "Notification delivery mechanism", "evolution_path": "Once async, can swap channels (email → SMS → push) without touching any upstream service" },
    { "area": "Customer API granularity", "evolution_path": "Focused endpoints enable BFF pattern or GraphQL federation later without backend changes" },
    { "area": "Reporting data pipeline", "evolution_path": "CDC or event-sourced read models can feed real-time dashboards, ML pipelines, or data lake" },
    { "area": "Job infrastructure", "evolution_path": "Queue-based jobs enable horizontal scaling of workers, priority queues, and rate limiting" }
  ],

  "rigidity_points": [
    { "area": "Order-Courier data separation", "reason": "Once split, cross-service joins are permanently gone. This is intentional — the constraint enforces proper boundaries. Must identify and migrate all cross-schema queries before splitting." },
    { "area": "Event schema contracts", "reason": "Once services communicate via events, the event schema becomes a published contract. Must version from day one." },
    { "area": "Notification Service API", "reason": "During migration, must maintain sync API alongside new async intake until all 5 callers migrate. Dual-write period is rigid." }
  ],

  "risks": [
    { "risk": "Shared database allows Courier schema changes to break Order Service (and vice versa) with zero warning at compile time", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Priority 1 remediation: split into separate databases with event-based sync" },
    { "risk": "Notification Service downtime cascades into 5 services simultaneously — every order, payment, and delivery flow blocks", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Priority 2 remediation: async event-driven notification intake with message broker" },
    { "risk": "Reporting Service's direct DB reads create invisible coupling — Order schema migrations can silently break reports", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Priority 3 remediation: CDC pipeline or event-sourced read model for Reporting" },
    { "risk": "/everything endpoint leaks payment methods and full order history to any caller, potential data exposure and performance bomb on large customers", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Priority 4 remediation: decompose into focused endpoints with proper authorization scoping" },
    { "risk": "HTTP-based background jobs are subject to load balancer timeouts, lack retry semantics, and can be accidentally triggered by monitoring health checks or vulnerability scanners", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Priority 5 remediation: migrate to proper job queue (SQS, Sidekiq, BullMQ, etc.)" },
    { "risk": "Big-bang database split could cause data loss or inconsistency during migration", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Use strangler fig pattern: dual-write phase → verify → cut over. Never big-bang." },
    { "risk": "Introducing a message broker adds operational complexity the team may not be ready for", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Start with a managed service (SQS, Cloud Pub/Sub, managed Kafka). Avoid self-hosted initially." }
  ],

  "implementation_guidance": {
    "recommended_approach": "Remediate in 5 phases, sequenced by risk severity and dependency order. Each phase is independently shippable.\n\nPHASE 1 — Shared Database Split (Order ↔ Courier) [Weeks 1-6]\n  Step 1: Catalog every cross-schema query between Order and Courier\n  Step 2: For each cross-schema dependency, create an API endpoint on the owning service\n  Step 3: Migrate consumers to use the API (strangler fig)\n  Step 4: Dual-write period: write to both schemas, read from API\n  Step 5: Verify data consistency, then cut Courier to its own PostgreSQL instance\n  Step 6: Remove legacy cross-schema access\n\nPHASE 2 — Async Notification [Weeks 4-8, overlaps Phase 1]\n  Step 1: Introduce message broker (managed service)\n  Step 2: Create NotificationRequested event schema (versioned from day 1)\n  Step 3: Notification Service subscribes to the event topic\n  Step 4: Migrate callers one-by-one: Order → Courier → Payment → Customer → Reporting\n  Step 5: Add DLQ monitoring and alerting\n  Step 6: Deprecate and remove synchronous notification endpoints\n\nPHASE 3 — Reporting Decoupling [Weeks 6-10]\n  Step 1: Identify all tables/columns Reporting reads from Order DB\n  Step 2: Implement CDC (Debezium) or event-based pipeline to replicate needed data into Reporting's own store\n  Step 3: Dual-read verification period\n  Step 4: Cut over Reporting to its own datastore\n  Step 5: Revoke Reporting's credentials to Order DB\n\nPHASE 4 — Customer API Decomposition [Weeks 8-12]\n  Step 1: Analyze callers of /everything — what fields does each actually use?\n  Step 2: Create focused endpoints: /profile, /addresses, /payment-methods, /order-history, /preferences\n  Step 3: Migrate callers one-by-one to focused endpoints\n  Step 4: Add deprecation header to /everything\n  Step 5: Monitor usage, then remove\n\nPHASE 5 — Job Infrastructure [Weeks 10-14]\n  Step 1: Introduce job queue infrastructure\n  Step 2: Wrap existing job logic in queue workers\n  Step 3: Migrate cron triggers to enqueue messages instead of HTTP calls\n  Step 4: Add monitoring, retry policies, DLQ\n  Step 5: Remove HTTP job endpoints",
    "files_to_create_or_modify": [
      "docs/adr/001-split-order-courier-database.md",
      "docs/adr/002-async-notification-events.md",
      "docs/adr/003-reporting-data-pipeline.md",
      "docs/adr/004-customer-api-decomposition.md",
      "docs/adr/005-job-queue-infrastructure.md",
      "order-service/src/api/ — new endpoints exposing data Courier currently reads via shared DB",
      "courier-service/src/clients/order-client.ts — API client replacing direct DB queries",
      "notification-service/src/consumers/notification-event-consumer.ts — event subscriber",
      "shared/events/notification-requested.schema.json — versioned event schema",
      "reporting-service/src/datastore/ — own read model store",
      "infrastructure/docker-compose.yml or terraform — broker + separate DB instances",
      "customer-service/src/api/v2/ — focused resource endpoints"
    ],
    "patterns_to_follow": "Strangler Fig for all migrations (never big-bang). Event-Carried State Transfer for cross-service data needs. Outbox Pattern for reliable event publishing. Anti-Corruption Layer when integrating with legacy endpoints during transition. Published Language for all event schemas (JSON Schema, versioned). Backend-for-Frontend if Customer API consumers have divergent needs.",
    "pitfalls_to_avoid": [
      "Big-bang database migration — always use dual-write/dual-read with verification",
      "Introducing sync HTTP calls to replace DB reads without considering latency impact — batch APIs or event-carried state transfer may be needed",
      "Publishing events without a schema registry or versioning strategy — you will break consumers within months",
      "Splitting the Notification Service sync API before all callers have migrated to async — will cause production failures",
      "Creating a message broker without DLQ monitoring — silent message loss is worse than sync failures",
      "Decomposing /everything without analyzing actual caller usage patterns — you may create endpoints nobody uses",
      "Treating the job endpoint migration as low priority — a vulnerability scanner hitting /api/v1/jobs/process-refunds could trigger unintended refund processing"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 85,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Anti-pattern identification — these are textbook and unambiguous",
      "Risk severity ranking — shared DB and sync fan-in are objectively the highest blast radius",
      "Remediation sequencing — dependency order is clear",
      "Pattern recommendations — strangler fig, event-driven, CDC are well-established for these exact problems"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact cross-schema query inventory — need codebase analysis to determine migration complexity",
      "Team capacity and infrastructure maturity — phases may need to stretch if team is small or lacks broker experience",
      "Reporting latency requirements — if near-real-time is required, CDC is mandatory; if daily is fine, simpler ETL works"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The team has access to managed cloud services (SQS/Pub-Sub/managed Kafka) — self-hosted broker changes the operational calculus",
      "Cross-schema queries between Order and Courier are identifiable and finite, not dynamically generated",
      "The /everything endpoint's consumers can be identified and migrated — no undocumented external consumers",
      "The cron job triggering HTTP endpoints is the only mechanism invoking background jobs — no other callers"
    ]
  }
}
```
